transcript:
Ninety small lights are orbiting a warm amber core, each one trailing a thin cyan line as it circles inward. Watch the first few seconds closely — that's the only time you'll clearly see the orb underneath. It's already thinning out.

Here's the quiet gut-punch, in the code: there's a variable called "cherished fades at," set to frame 300. After that point, the center simply never gets redrawn again — it's not being destroyed, it's just not being tended to anymore, while everything else keeps circling and layering on top of it. And down in the render loop, there's a comment that says the fade "can't keep up" — the void's own alpha is so low that old trails persist longer than they fade, so the accumulation always wins.

There's also a slow-building variable called "escalation," creeping toward a ceiling of 3.2 — the devotees' urgency increasing forever, never resolving, never panicking, just... more.

The devotees never stop caring. That's exactly the problem — the orb doesn't die from neglect, it disappears from attention.
